home.tagline: Train your poker intuition — think in ranges, not exact numbers.
home.proFeatNote: Spaced repetition for hands you missed — fix your weak spots systematically.

login.title: Login
login.invalidToken: This verification link is invalid or has expired. Please request a new one.
login.resetSuccess: Your password has been reset. Please log in.

registerToUnlock.subtitle: Registered feature
registerToUnlock.body1: Turn and River training, as well as Pot Odds, are available for registered users. Registration is free.
registerToUnlock.body2: With a free account you also unlock Intermediate difficulty and your progress is saved across sessions.

leakInfo.title: Equity Leaks
leakInfo.body1: The Leak Trainer analyzes your past hands and automatically schedules the ones you struggled with for review — using spaced repetition, similar to how flashcard apps like Anki work.
leakInfo.body2: Every hand you get wrong during training becomes a <leak>leak</leak>. The system brings it back after 1 hour, then 24 hours, then 7 days. Get it right three times in a row and the leak is fixed.
leakInfo.guestNote: Leak Training is available for Pro members. Register for free to unlock Turn & River training, or go Pro for full access.
leakInfo.registeredNote: Upgrade your account to Pro to unlock Leak Training, Advanced and Expert difficulty levels, and all future modules.

train.handsToActivate: Play {count} more hands to activate the rolling score window
train.noHandsAvailable: No hands available. Please run the generate script to fill the hand pool.

moduleExplanation.handVsHand1: You see two specific hands and estimate the <equity>equity</equity> of your hand against your opponent's — preflop, before any community cards are dealt.
moduleExplanation.handVsHand2: Rarely directly relevant in practice since you usually don't see your opponent's hand until showdown. Still important for understanding relative hand strengths and building a foundation for range thinking.
moduleExplanation.handVsHand3: Equity shifters to watch for: suited cards (≈ +3%), connected cards (straights possible), gaps in connectors (reduced straight potential), blockers (your cards reduce opponent's outs), shared ranks (split pot possible — shifts equity toward 50%), and suit domination (both suited in the same suit — the lower flush draw is nearly worthless).
moduleExplanation.handVsHand4: Fun fact: The so-called "coinflip" (pair vs two overcards) is not really a coinflip — the pair is ahead ≈ 53% to 47%. That's a 6 percentage point difference, which adds up over many hands.
moduleExplanation.preflop1: You estimate your <equity>equity</equity> against the villain's <range>range</range> — expressed as <topX>Top X%</topX> of their possible starting hands.
moduleExplanation.preflop2: Equity below 50% doesn't automatically mean fold — you need pot odds for that. The goal is building number sense for <eqRange>equity vs a range</eqRange>, not exact calculation.
moduleExplanation.flop1: The flop changes <equity>equity</equity> drastically — draws, pair outs, and board textures now play a major role.
moduleExplanation.flop2: The villain's <topX>Top X% range</topX> is a simplification: in real games you'd adjust it post-flop. Here you're still training a valuable intuition — how strong your hand is against a typical opening range.
moduleExplanation.turn1: On the turn only one card remains — <equity>equity</equity> is more concrete but also more volatile: a single river out can flip it completely.
moduleExplanation.turn2: The shown <range>range</range> is the villain's preflop opening range. In a real game it would already be narrowed significantly by the turn — here you're practicing the fundamentals before working with range adjustments.
moduleExplanation.river1: On the river there are no more cards — <equity>equity</equity> is either 0% or 100%. What you're training here is: how often is my hand ahead against a typical villain <range>range</range>?
moduleExplanation.river2: The preflop range is of course the furthest from reality here. Still, this training sharpens your feel for how well or poorly your hand performs on a given board.

combinedPotOdds.title: Hand vs Range + Pot Odds
combinedPotOdds.breakevenZone: Breakeven zone — both choices are defensible
combinedPotOdds.shouldHaveCalledDetail: Your equity ({equity}%) exceeds the required equity — calling would have been more profitable.
combinedPotOdds.shouldHaveFoldedDetail: Your equity ({equity}%) doesn't cover a profitable call — folding would have been better.

guestBanner.message: You are training as a guest. Register to save your progress permanently.

rangeTraining.selectHands: Select all hands you would open from this position.
rangeTraining.explanation1: You see a table position and 5 starting hands. Mark which ones you would open (raise with) from that position.
rangeTraining.explanation2: Opening ranges vary by position. Under the Gun (first to act) needs much stronger hands because many players are still to act. The Button (last to act) can open very wide because positional advantage compensates for weaker hands.
rangeTraining.explanation3: These are standard TAG (Tight-Aggressive) opening ranges for 6-max. Real ranges vary by player type and table dynamics — but mastering these fundamentals is essential before adjusting.
